prophecy-and-persia.title: Prophecy & Persia
prophecy-and-persia.existing_en: Welcome to Prophecy and Persia: Cyrus, Islam and the Imam Mahdi. In front of you stands the towering stone gateway of Persepolis, its colossal winged bull-men keeping watch as they have for millennia. This exhibition follows a single remarkable thread through history, in three parts. First, we meet Cyrus the Great, the Persian emperor whom the Holy Quran may call Dhul-Qarnain. Then we witness the Rise of Islam and Persia's place within it. Finally, we come to the Promised Messiah, the awaited reformer of Persian origin. This audio guide will accompany you at every board, and is available in several languages. Take your time, and let the journey begin.

cyrus.title: Cyrus
cyrus.existing_en: You are entering Section One: Cyrus. Its theme is unravelling the true identity of Dhul-Qarnain, the two-horned king mentioned in the Holy Quran. Over the boards ahead, you will meet Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian Empire, and weigh the case that he, rather than Alexander, was the righteous figure the Quran describes. Watch for his conquests to the west, the east and the north, his famous cylinder, and the wall he raised against Gog and Magog. Let us begin with the promise of the Holy Quran itself.

promise-of-the-holy-quran.title: The Promise of the Holy Quran
promise-of-the-holy-quran.existing_en: Take a moment to consider the Holy Quran, shown here in flowing Arabic calligraphy against deep blue. Islam teaches that God is the Lord of every realm, every people and every nation. No one people is inherently superior to another; all humans are equal before God. What raises a person is not lineage but righteousness, and righteousness comes through following the prophets. The Quran tells us, in chapter 16, verse 37: 'And We did raise among every people a Messenger with the teaching, Worship Allah and shun the Evil One.' Each nation received guidance, yet over time religions were corrupted. At last humanity was ready for a universal message, Islam, for all peoples and all time. And should its spirit ever be lost by the Muslims, God Himself promised to revive it. As the Quran declares, in chapter 15, verse 10: 'Verily, We Ourself have sent down this Exhortation, and most surely We will be its Guardian.' As you will discover, that story of renewal is bound up with one particular people: the Persians.

ancient-persia-zoroastrianism.title: Ancient Persia & Zoroastrianism
ancient-persia-zoroastrianism.existing_en: In front of you rise the ruins of ancient Persepolis at golden hour. The name Persia comes from Parsa, an ancient region in what is now the Iranian province of Fars. Its people called themselves the Arya, meaning the noble or the civilised, and named their homeland Iran. Fars was the heart of Cyrus the Great's Achaemenid dynasty, and as the empire spread, the name Persian came to embrace many peoples. Long before Cyrus, a great prophetic figure arose here: Zarathustra, later known as Zoroaster. Commissioned by Ahura Mazda, the One True God, he called his people away from worshipping many gods, and was bitterly opposed by the priests and princes of his land. Forced to migrate for his faith, he lamented, 'To what land shall I turn, and whither turning shall I go? For my kinsmen and my peers have deserted me.' Yet in the end he was victorious, and his spiritual message became a worldly kingdom through one of Persia's greatest sons: Cyrus the Great.

cyrus-ii-persian-emperor.title: Cyrus II, Persian Emperor
cyrus-ii-persian-emperor.existing_en: This carved relief from Persepolis shows a procession of Persian and Median nobles. Cyrus was born into a world on fire. For three centuries the fearsome Neo-Assyrian empire dominated the region. In seven hundred and twenty-two BCE it enslaved the Northern Kingdom of Israel; and though Assyria itself fell in six hundred and twelve BCE, the Babylonians rose in its place, destroying Solomon's Temple around five hundred and eighty-seven BCE. Into this turmoil came Cyrus, born around six hundred BCE. Inheriting the small Persian state of Anshan, he overthrew the cruel Median king Astyages and united Media and Persia, laying the foundation of the great Achaemenid Empire. The prophet Nahum had foretold Assyria's downfall, and it came to pass within decades. The exiled Jews wept by the rivers of Babylon, remembering Zion. Their saviour, whom the Bible would call Messiah, would not be an Israelite but a Zoroastrian. And it is the Holy Quran that establishes his divine support, through the enigmatic story of Dhul-Qarnain, the two-horned king.

the-cyrus-cylinder.title: The Cyrus Cylinder
the-cyrus-cylinder.existing_en: Behind you stands a replica of the Cyrus Cylinder, inscribed around five hundred and thirty-nine BCE in the native Akkadian tongue to record the conquest of Babylon. In Cyrus's own voice it declares: 'I am Cyrus, King of the world. When I entered Babylon, I did not allow anyone to terrorise the land. I strove for peace in Babylon and in all her cities. Marduk, the great lord, rejoiced at my good deeds, and he pronounced a sweet blessing over me.' Written by the Babylonian elite themselves, it is one of the earliest surviving records of Cyrus's pioneering religious tolerance.

who-was-dhul-qarnain.title: Who Was Dhul-Qarnain?
who-was-dhul-qarnain.existing_en: Here two ancient maps sit side by side: the empire of Alexander the Great, and the Achaemenid Persian Empire around five hundred BCE. The eighteenth chapter of the Holy Quran traces the story of the Christian world, and near its close tells how Dhul-Qarnain held back the marauding peoples of Gog and Magog. But who was he? Most Muslims believe he was Alexander the Great; the Caliphs of the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community, however, make the case for Cyrus. Consider three clues. The title Dhul-Qarnain means one who possesses two horns, or who spans two generations; Cyrus united two kingdoms and lived a long life, while Alexander died at just thirty-two. Dhul-Qarnain journeyed west, then east; Cyrus did exactly this, while Alexander expanded only eastward. And Dhul-Qarnain was a righteous, tolerant figure; Cyrus ushered in an unprecedented era of religious freedom, whereas Alexander was a brilliant but profligate pagan conqueror. The evidence, they argue, points firmly to Cyrus.

the-story-of-dhul-qarnain.title: The Story of Dhul-Qarnain
the-story-of-dhul-qarnain.existing_en: Before you is the Quranic account of Dhul-Qarnain, from chapter 18, verses 85 to 100. Take a moment as we read from it. 'And they ask thee about Dhul-Qarnain. Say, I will certainly recite to you something of his story. We established him in the earth and gave him the means to accomplish everything. Then he followed a certain way. Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it setting in a pool of murky water, and near it he found a people.' He journeyed onward to the rising of the sun, and then to a place between two mountains, where a people begged his help against Gog and Magog. He raised a mighty rampart of iron and molten copper that they could neither scale nor dig through, saying, 'This is a mercy from my Lord.' Yet he warned that when the promise of his Lord comes to pass, even this wall would be broken into pieces. This single passage, as the exhibition shows, holds the key to Cyrus's story, and to prophecies still to unfold.

persian-king-israelite-savior.title: Persian King, Israelite Saviour
persian-king-israelite-savior.existing_en: This classical painting shows the exiled people of Judah in mourning captivity. The Quran says Dhul-Qarnain journeyed to the setting of the sun, finding it setting in a pool of murky water. Remarkably, it was a king of western Turkey who attacked Cyrus in five hundred and forty-six BCE. Cyrus marched from Iran and defeated an army twice the size of his own, in a region near modern Izmir still famous today for its hot, muddy mineral springs, just as the Quran describes. He then turned to Babylon, where the Jews lay in exile. In five hundred and thirty-nine BCE he defeated the Babylonians, then diverted the River Euphrates so his men could wade into the city by night, taking it almost without battle. There he punished the guilty and showed kindness to others. He ended the Chaldean dynasty, reversed forced labour, restored the temples of all, decreed the rebuilding of Solomon's Temple, and let the Israelites return home. Without Cyrus, the faith we now call Judaism might not exist as we know it.

the-prophecy-of-isaiah.title: The Prophecy of Isaiah
the-prophecy-of-isaiah.existing_en: This board presents a prophecy from the Book of Isaiah, chapter 45, verses 1 to 6, spoken long before Cyrus was born. It reads: 'Thus says the Lord to His anointed one, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have held, to subdue nations before him. I will go before you and make the crooked places straight; I will break in pieces the gates of bronze and cut the bars of iron. I will give you the treasures of darkness, that you may know that I, the Lord, who call you by your name, am the God of Israel.' The Bible names Cyrus its anointed one, its Messiah, and God calls him by name, though he had not yet known Him.

false-claims-of-cyrus-death.title: False Claims of Cyrus' Death
false-claims-of-cyrus-death.existing_en: In front of you stands the stepped stone Tomb of Cyrus the Great at Pasargadae. After securing his western border, Cyrus advanced east against the Massagetae, harsh nomads living in what is now Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan, a people so exposed to the fierce Central Asian sun that, Herodotus tells us, they even worshipped it. The Quran describes him reaching the rising of the sun, upon a people with no shelter against it, and adds that God encompassed with His knowledge all that was with him. Greek historians give at least three accounts of Cyrus's death. Herodotus, choosing the tale that best fit his theme of moral downfall, says he was beheaded here by the queen Tomyris. Ctesias says he was wounded and died later at home. Xenophon reports that he was victorious and lived a long, full life. The Quran aligns with Xenophon, continuing Cyrus's story beyond this campaign. And his tomb here at Pasargadae, over two thousand miles from the Massagetae, bears silent witness that he did not die on the eastern front.

cyrus-confronts-gog-and-magog.title: Cyrus Confronts Gog & Magog
cyrus-confronts-gog-and-magog.existing_en: Before you is a map of the Achaemenid Empire at its greatest extent, with a red arrow pointing north to the Caucasus. The Quran tells how Dhul-Qarnain travelled to a place between two barriers, where he found a people who could scarcely understand a word of his language. They begged his help against Gog and Magog, who were spreading disorder. Three clues identify them. They lived beneath two great mountain barriers; they did not naturally speak his tongue; and they were under attack. Look north of Cyrus's empire and you find the towering Caucasus, stretching from the Black Sea to the Caspian. The people below were ancestors of today's Georgians, whose language is a rare isolate, unrelated to any other, which Cyrus could not have understood. And their attackers? The Scythians, fierce horse-riding archers descended from the Yamnaya of the Russian steppe, whose descendants would spread west to become the Celts, and others the Slavic and Russian peoples.

the-wall-of-cyrus.title: The Wall of Cyrus the Great
the-wall-of-cyrus.existing_en: Look at the green mountain valley before you, and the insets of the ancient Wall of Derbent and the Darial Gorge. In the Quran, the people offer Dhul-Qarnain tribute to build a barrier, but he replies that the power God has given him is better, asking only for their strength: 'Bring me blocks of iron.' Herodotus records that the Colchians of western Georgia paid no fixed tax, but brought gifts of manpower every four years, just as the Quran suggests. The Quran describes the iron made red as fire, then sealed with molten qitr, a word meaning molten metal. Remarkably, at Cyrus's royal centre of Pasargadae, builders joined stone blocks without mortar by pouring molten lead over iron clamps. Two routes let the Scythians through: the Darial Gorge, and the pass between the mountains and the sea. Both were guarded by great Persian fortifications, and either, or both, may first have been raised by Cyrus himself to keep the Scythians out.

the-reality-of-faith.title: The Reality of Faith
the-reality-of-faith.existing_en: This board closes our section on Cyrus with a verse on the reality of faith, from the Holy Quran, chapter 49, verse 14: 'O mankind, We have created you from male and female; and We have divided you into tribes and sub-tribes for the sake of easy recognition. Verily, the most honoured among you in the sight of Allah is he who is the most righteous among you. Surely, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware.' Difference of nation and tribe, it teaches, is only for recognition. True nobility lies not in birth but in righteousness, the very quality that marked Cyrus the Great.

the-rise-of-islam.title: The Rise of Islam
the-rise-of-islam.existing_en: You are entering Section Two: The Rise of Islam, or, as this board names it, the Second Persian Revolution. Ahead, you will see how the world had fallen into darkness by the seventh century, and how a new dawn broke in the deserts of Arabia with the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. You will meet a beloved Persian companion, witness prophecies fulfilled against mighty empires, and see how the people of Persia embraced Islam and enriched it. Let us begin in an age of darkness.

an-age-of-darkness.title: An Age of Darkness
an-age-of-darkness.existing_en: Before you a dark sea breaks against the shore under a deep blue night. By the seventh century, Persia was no longer the tolerant empire of Cyrus. After Alexander destroyed the Achaemenids around three hundred and thirty BCE, Zoroastrianism itself changed: one supreme God gave way to many spirits, fire and sun worship grew, and priests persecuted minorities. Persia was locked in endless war with Rome, whose rulers brutalised dissenting Christians and crushed their people with taxes. Arabia was no different: tribal warfare raged, baby girls were sometimes killed at birth, and slavery was rife. As the Quran says, in chapter 30, verse 42, 'Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what men's hands have wrought.' Yet it was into this very darkness that something new emerged. In the words of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, hundreds of thousands of the spiritually dead were revived within a brief period, and the world underwent a revolution that had never been seen or heard of before.

a-new-dawn.title: A New Dawn
a-new-dawn.existing_en: A glowing sunrise rises over the sea before you. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, was born in Mecca in five hundred and seventy CE. Orphaned young, he grew renowned for his piety and trustworthiness. At the age of twenty-five he married Khadija, a merchant-woman of forty, and often withdrew to the Cave of Hira to pray for days on end. In six hundred and ten CE, revelation began, and it continued for some twenty-three years until his passing in six hundred and thirty-two. Its core was simple: there is no God but Allah, and Muhammad is His Messenger. In a world that had lost God's unity to Trinity and idolatry, Islam restored pure oneness. The Quran declares, in chapter 2, verse 256, 'Allah is He save Whom none is worthy of worship, the Ever-Living, the Self-Subsisting and All-Sustaining.' Once, the angel Gabriel appeared in human form and questioned the Prophet about Islam, faith and righteousness, teaching that you should worship God as if you behold Him, and if you cannot, then knowing that He watches you.

the-prophecy-of-rome.title: The Prophecy of Rome
the-prophecy-of-rome.existing_en: Before you is the weathered stone bust of a Roman emperor. In these years the early Muslims were fiercely persecuted; many were tortured, and many martyred. As the Persian emperor Khosrow the Second stormed to victory over Rome, taking Damascus, then Jerusalem and the True Cross, Rome seemed finished. Yet in six hundred and sixteen CE the Quran foretold, in chapter 30, verses 3 to 5: 'The Romans have been defeated, in the land nearby, and they, after their defeat, will be victorious. In a few years the believers will rejoice.' The Arabic promised victory within three to nine years, and joy for the Muslims. In six hundred and twenty-four CE, at the Valley of Badr, a great Meccan army marched against the Prophet, peace be upon him. The night before, he was told of victory, and so it was. On that very day, word arrived that Rome had at last defeated Persia. Every arm of the prophecy was fulfilled at once.

the-persian-companion.title: The Persian Companion
the-persian-companion.existing_en: Palm trees rise before a mosque here, a fitting image for Salman the Persian. Born near Isfahan and raised a Zoroastrian firekeeper, Salman was drawn to a Christian church, and when he sought to convert his father shackled him at home. Escaping to Syria, he journeyed from teacher to teacher in search of true righteousness, until his last mentor, on his deathbed, foretold a new prophet coming to the Arabs, in a land of palm trees between two bare tracts, bearing three marks. Betrayed and sold into slavery, Salman reached Medina, waited, and then tested the newcomer: he gave charity but would not accept it, ate of a gift, and bore a mark on his back. Finding the Prophet true, Salman never left his side again. Later, his knowledge of a defensive trench saved the Muslims from a vast army. When companions disputed whether he was of Mecca or Medina, the Prophet, peace be upon him, said, 'Salman is from among us, the People of the Household.'

faith-in-the-pleiades.title: Faith in the Pleiades
faith-in-the-pleiades.existing_en: This board records a moment narrated by Abu Huraira, in the collection of Bukhari. One day, as the companions sat together and the chapter of Jumu'ah was revealed, one of them asked the Prophet who was meant by the words, 'and among others from among them who have not yet joined them.' Salman the Persian was sitting among them. The Prophet, peace be upon him, placed his hand upon Salman and said: 'If Faith were to go up to the Pleiades, a man from among these would surely find it.' It is a promise that, as this exhibition unfolds, points across the centuries to a reformer of Persian descent.

persian-downfall.title: Persian Downfall
persian-downfall.existing_en: Before you, a row of carved Persian heads gazes out from Persepolis. Commissioned to carry his message to the world, the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, wrote to both the Roman emperor Heraclius and the Persian emperor Khosrow the Second, inviting them to Islam. His letter to Persia was gracious, opening in the name of Allah and calling the ruler to salvation. Heraclius received the message with respect. Khosrow, incensed, tore the letter to pieces, saying, 'He is my servant and he addresses me in this way.' Hearing this, the Prophet declared, 'Now these people shall themselves be shattered to pieces.' Khosrow then ordered the Prophet's arrest. But when his emissaries came, the Prophet told them one morning, 'My Lord, the God of power, has killed his master last night.' Word soon arrived: Khosrow had been murdered by his own son, on that very night. And so, as the Prophet foretold, the Persian kingdom was destroyed at once, while Rome was granted some respite.

islamic-tolerance.title: Islamic Tolerance
islamic-tolerance.existing_en: The domes and minarets of a great mosque stand silhouetted against the sunset before you. The Quran declares, in chapter 2, verse 257, 'There is no compulsion in religion,' and in chapter 18, verse 30, 'let him who will, believe, and let him who will, disbelieve.' After the Prophet's passing in six hundred and thirty-two CE, a newly united Arabia astonished the world by overcoming both Rome and Persia. Yet Muslim rule, especially in its first three centuries, protected its religious minorities; the tolerant spirit of Cyrus was enlarged and perfected. In the tenth century, fire-temples still stood in almost every Persian province. In Muslim Spain, Jews who had suffered under the Visigoths enjoyed a cultural renaissance; the caliph Abd al-Rahman the Third even had a Jewish foreign minister and a Christian bishop as envoy. As Sir Thomas Arnold observed, many Persians embraced Islam eagerly, finding in it a brotherhood of equals and echoes of their older faith.

persian-renaissance.title: Persian Renaissance
persian-renaissance.existing_en: Above you glows a turquoise-tiled Persian dome. The empires of Persia reached far beyond modern Iran, into Arabia, Central Asia, the Levant, Afghanistan, the Indian subcontinent and the Caucasus. Countless peoples became Persianate in culture without being ethnically Iranian, and embraced Islam wholeheartedly, until Persian became the language of scholarship across much of the Muslim world. Though Iran is associated with Shia Islam today, most Iranians were Sunni before the Safavid dynasty made Twelver Shiism the state religion in fifteen hundred and one. Yet Sunni and Shia alike awaited the Imam Mahdi, a great reformer of the Latter Days. Persia gave Islam an extraordinary array of minds: Imam Bukhari and Imam Muslim, compilers of hadith; Al-Khwarizmi, the father of algebra; the physicians Al-Razi and Ibn Sina; the philosopher Al-Farabi; the polymath Al-Biruni; the scholar-saint Al-Ghazali; and the beloved poet Rumi. The renaissance of Persia became a gift to the whole world.

o-sons-of-persia.title: O Sons of Persia
o-sons-of-persia.existing_en: This board carries a short but stirring revelation given to Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad. Twice it repeats a single command, then names those it addresses: 'Hold fast to unity. Hold fast to unity. O sons of Persia.' It is a call across the centuries to the people of Persia, urging them to hold together in faith. As this final section will reveal, that call was bound up with the coming of a reformer of Persian origin, the Promised Messiah, to whom we now turn.

the-promised-messiah.title: The Promised Messiah
the-promised-messiah.existing_en: You are entering the final section: The Promised Messiah, the Imam Mahdi of Persian origin. Before you is a portrait of a bearded man in a turban. In the boards ahead, you will learn how a new dark age fell upon Islam in the nineteenth century, and how one man, Mirza Ghulam Ahmad of Qadian, arose in answer. You will see how the ancient story of Dhul-Qarnain was fulfilled a second time, in prophecy and in a wall of faith. Let us begin with a new dark age.

a-new-dark-age.title: A New Dark Age
a-new-dark-age.existing_en: A slender crescent moon hangs against the black sky before you. By the nineteenth century, Islam was under deadly attack. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, had foretold such a time: nothing would remain of Islam except its name, and nothing of the Quran except its inscription, with splendid mosques empty of guidance. In British India, Christian missionaries were converting Muslims by the thousand, and a new Hindu movement, the Arya Samaj, was attacking the Prophet and the Quran. Islam was crying out for a saviour. One man came forth: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad of Qadian. He wrote that the Promised Messiah would be made to stand against Satan in a final, mighty struggle between good and evil, in which every trick of the evil one would be exhausted; and that after a heavy fight, the Messiah of God would drive back the powers of darkness, and God's glory would be proclaimed on earth. 'I am that Messiah,' he declared, 'let him who will, accept me.'

the-persian-messiah.title: The Persian Messiah
the-persian-messiah.existing_en: Before you is an ornate Mughal-style palace facade. The Quran speaks, in chapter 62, verse 4, of a Messenger raised among the unlettered people, and of others from among them who have not yet joined them. When this was revealed, the Prophet, peace be upon him, laid his hand on Salman the Persian and said, 'If Faith were to go up to the Pleiades, a man from among these would surely find it.' The family of Hazrat Ahmad, who lived from eighteen thirty-five to nineteen hundred and eight, was of Persian origin, its ancestors settled near Samarkand in a deeply Persianate land. He received revelations naming his Persian descent, one calling him a man of Persian descent whose book is the sword of Ali. Living quietly in prayer and study, he was moved to defend Islam publicly, and in the eighteen-eighties he produced his great work, Barahin-e-Ahmadiyya, which even a future opponent called unique in the history of Islam.

prophecy-of-the-promised-messiah.title: A Prophecy Fulfilled
prophecy-of-the-promised-messiah.existing_en: This board sets out a prophecy fulfilled, in the words of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad. He recalls the Holy Prophet's foretelling that a time would come when the Muslims would strongly resemble the Jews, and that then a man of Persian origin would be raised to teach them the faith, one who, had faith ascended to the Pleiades, would have brought it down from that height. Just as the Messiah, son of Mary, came fourteen centuries after Moses, so, he explains, when about fourteen hundred years had passed after the Holy Prophet and the Muslims had developed the same failings as the Jews, God sent, in His perfect power, the like of the Messiah as a teacher of faith.

gog-and-magog.title: Gog & Magog
gog-and-magog.existing_en: Silhouetted soldiers advance through smoke and light above you. The Quran warned, in chapter 18, verses 99 and 100, that Dhul-Qarnain's wall was a mercy, but 'when the promise of my Lord shall come to pass, He will break it into pieces.' As we have seen, Gog and Magog were violent tribes pressing down from Europe and Russia into the lands of the Persians and Arabs. In eighteen hundred and one, Russia took the Darial Gorge; in eighteen thirteen, the Derbent fortifications fell too, and for over a century Russia used these gateways to attack Iran. The warning was fulfilled. Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad taught that the very names Gog and Magog derive from a word meaning fire, foretelling a people whose ships, trains and machines would run by fire, and who would excel all others in harnessing it: the people of the West. Their spiritual face he called the Dajjal, whose two jaws, he said, were the Christian missionaries and the European philosophers.

dhul-qarnain-revealed.title: Dhul-Qarnain
dhul-qarnain-revealed.existing_en: A softly lit open Quran rests before you. The Promised Messiah revealed a hidden truth: that historical events narrated in the Quran also foretell events to come. So the story of Dhul-Qarnain, which pointed to the great Persian emperor of the past, points also to the Promised Persian Messiah of the future. Hazrat Ahmad declared, 'I have come as Dhul-Qarnain for this Ummah,' one who witnesses two centuries, as he did in every calendar in use. He said he had been given means to spread the truth that were unavailable to any earlier prophet, so that a piece of writing could now reach the whole world within forty days. The setting sun, he explained, is the West, sunk in spiritual darkness and drinking from a foul pool of belief; the rising sun in the East is a people scorched by their worship of outward form and by extremism. To both, the Sun of Truth would come.

the-wall-of-islam.title: The Wall of Islam
the-wall-of-islam.existing_en: Before you an open Quran glows on a wooden stand. The Promised Messiah described how Dhul-Qarnain would reach a place between two barriers, a time when the powers of darkness and of the state together would present an awe-striking spectacle. Beneath them he would find a third people, victims of false belief, who would struggle to understand him, but who in the end would attain guidance. To them he gave a charge: 'Bring me slabs of iron,' hold fast to my teachings and build yourselves into a wall of iron against the enemy; then blow fire into the iron, feeding the flames of love for God until you take on the Divine complexion. From such a community, he said, God would gather all the rightly inclined upon one faith, until there was one Shepherd and one flock. As this board shows, what Cyrus fulfilled in stone and conquest, Hazrat Ahmad fulfilled in spirit: two centuries joined, light brought to the west, extremism resisted in the east, and a community raised as a barrier against the Dajjal.

the-palace-prophecy.title: The Palace Prophecy
the-palace-prophecy.existing_en: Before you is a grand Persian mosque courtyard at dusk. On the fifteenth of January, nineteen hundred and six, the Promised Messiah received a striking revelation: 'The Palace of Chosroes has been shaken.' It concerned Iran's ruling Qajar monarchy, which had enjoyed over a century of absolute power with the backing of Imperial Russia. Within two years the prophecy stirred to life. After the Shah's death in nineteen hundred and seven, his successor suspended the constitution and shelled the parliament in Tehran; but pro-constitutional forces rose up, captured the city in nineteen hundred and nine, and forced him to abdicate. The palace of Chosroes had truly been shaken. Another revelation, from nineteen hundred and five, had declared the purpose of his advent: 'Gather together all the Muslims on the face of the earth so that they should unite in one faith.' By his call, he said, those who had been Muslims in name began to become Muslims in truth, some four hundred thousand of them.

a-blessed-dream.title: A Blessed Dream
a-blessed-dream.existing_en: This final board shares a blessed dream of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad. He relates: 'I saw in a blessed dream a group of sincere believers and just and righteous kings, some of whom belonged to this country, some to Arabia, some to Iran, some to Syria, some to Turkey, and some to other regions of which I am not aware.' Then, he says, Allah told him: 'These people will affirm your righteousness and will believe in you and will pray for you. I shall bestow great blessings upon you, so much so that kings will seek blessings from your garments.' It is a fitting close to our journey, from Cyrus of Persia to the Promised Messiah of Persian origin.
